title: Control Flow
summary: Make decisions in your code using if, else if, and else statements to handle different scenarios

sections.title: Control Flow Is Like a Fork in the Road
sections.content: Imagine walking down a path and coming to a fork. If it is raining, you take the covered route; otherwise, you take the scenic path. Control flow statements do the same thing for your code. The 'if' keyword checks a condition (a boolean expression) and runs a block of code only when that condition is true. 'else if' checks a second condition if the first was false. 'else' catches everything that did not match any condition. This lets your program behave differently based on data — like applying different shipping costs for orders above or below $50.

sections.title: Determining a Student's Grade
sections.content: Uses if-else if-else to assign letter grades based on a numeric score

sections.title: Nested Conditions and the Ternary Operator
sections.content: Sometimes you need to check a condition inside another condition — like checking if a user is logged in before checking if they are an admin. This is called nesting. Be careful: deeply nested code (3+ levels) becomes hard to read, like Russian nesting dolls. For simple two-way decisions, TypeScript offers the ternary operator: 'condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse'. It is a compact if-else that fits on one line. For example, 'const fee = isMember ? 0 : 5.99;' means members pay nothing, non-members pay $5.99. Use ternary for short conditions and if-else for complex logic.

sections.title: Assignment (=) Instead of Comparison (===) in Conditions
sections.content: The #1 beginner mistake is writing 'if (score = 100)' with a single equals sign instead of 'if (score === 100)'. The single equals assigns the value and always evaluates to true (since the assigned value is truthy). Wrong approach: 'if (isLoggedIn = true)' — this sets isLoggedIn to true instead of checking it. Correct approach: 'if (isLoggedIn === true)' or simply 'if (isLoggedIn)'. TypeScript may warn about this, but the habit of double-checking your condition operators will save you hours of debugging.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A movie theater charges different ticket prices based on age: under 12 pays $8, 12 to 64 pays $14, and 65+ pays $10. Write a program that takes an age variable and prints the correct ticket price. Make sure a negative age doesn't break your logic.
hints: What is the first condition you should check — the special case or the normal range?
Should you use if, else if, or else for each age bracket?
What happens if the age is 0 or negative — should you guard against that?
